past_medical_history: Prolonged febrile seizure at age 2 years requiring emergency department evaluation
Recurrent otitis media in childhood with bilateral myringotomy tubes placed at age 5
Mild obesity, managed with lifestyle modifications
family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 50
Maternal grandmother with epilepsy, started in her twenties, treated with phenobarbital for decades
No family history of brain tumors, stroke, or neurodegenerative disease

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinker, approximately one glass of wine per month
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies any illicit drug use, denies marijuana use
social_history.living_situation: Lives with his fiancee in a rented apartment in a suburban neighborhood
social_history.driving: Holds a valid driver's license, commutes 20 minutes to work by car daily
social_history.exercise: Practices yoga three times per week, occasional jogging

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent with normal prosody, no word-finding difficulties. Naming intact for high- and low-frequency items. Repetition and comprehension intact. Registration 3/3, recall 3/3 at five minutes. Able to spell WORLD backward correctly. Patient appears somewhat anxious but cooperative. Affect is appropriate.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light bilaterally (4 mm to 2 mm). Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick in all three divisions bilaterally. Facial movements symmetric with equal forehead wrinkling and nasolabial fold depth. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Uvula midline. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline, no atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone throughout. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups of upper and lower extremities bilaterally, including deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip, finger abductors, hip flexors, quadriceps, hamstrings, tibialis anterior, and gastrocnemius. No pronator drift. No adventitious movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, proprioception, and vibratory sense intact in all extremities. Graphesthesia and stereognosis intact bilaterally. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing accurate bilaterally without intention tremor or dysmetria. Heel-to-shin smooth bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact and symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait normal with symmetric stride length and arm swing. Tandem gait intact without difficulty. Romberg sign negative.
neurological_exam.additional: A healing 5 mm lateral tongue bite wound is noted on the left side, consistent with the witnessed seizure 8 hours prior. No bruising or other injuries from the event.

patient.chief_complaint: Witnessed generalized seizure with preceding episodes of an unusual smell and a churning sensation in the stomach over the past several months
patient.history_present_illness:
Mr. Kapoor is a 42-year-old left-handed elementary school teacher who was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at his workplace. According to a colleague who witnessed the event, the patient was seated at his desk during a planning period when he suddenly stopped writing, appeared to stare vacantly at the wall, and then began making repetitive chewing movements. His left hand was noted to pick at his clothing in a purposeless manner. The colleague attempted to get his attention by calling his name and tapping his shoulder, but the patient was unresponsive. After approximately 20 seconds of this behavior, the patient's body stiffened and he fell from his chair onto the floor, developing rhythmic jerking of all four extremities lasting roughly two minutes. He was placed on his side by another staff member who was CPR trained. A small amount of blood-tinged saliva was noted at the mouth. He was incontinent of urine during the episode. Emergency medical services arrived within eight minutes. On their arrival, the convulsive activity had stopped and the patient was in a post-ictal state, confused, drowsy, and unable to follow commands. He was transported to the hospital and gradually became more alert over the next 45 minutes.

Upon questioning in the emergency department, the patient recalls that immediately before the event he perceived an intense, unpleasant smell that he describes as similar to burning rubber or chemicals. This olfactory sensation was accompanied by a strong churning or fluttering feeling in his upper abdomen that seemed to rise toward his chest. He estimates these sensations lasted approximately 10 to 15 seconds before he lost awareness. He reports that he has been experiencing these identical episodes of the unusual smell and abdominal churning approximately two to three times per month for the past eight months. Each episode typically lasts 20 to 40 seconds. During most of these episodes he has been able to continue what he was doing, though he feels momentarily confused afterward. On two occasions his fiance observed his staring blankly for about half a minute and not responding when he spoke to her, after which he appeared dazed for a minute or two. He did not seek medical attention for these episodes because he attributed them to stress related to wedding planning and believed they might be panic attacks. He denies any history of prior convulsions. There has been no recent head trauma, illness, fever, sleep deprivation, or significant change in his medications. He denies alcohol or substance use around the time of the event.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz, symmetric, well-organized alpha rhythm attenuating with eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep transients including vertex waves and sleep spindles captured during brief drowsiness and stage II sleep

findings.frequency: Frequent, occurring approximately every 1-2 minutes during wakefulness, increasing during drowsiness and stage II sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8 with field extending to T4 and F4, spike duration 30-50 ms followed by 200-300 ms slow wave component
findings.state: Present in wakefulness and sleep, maximal during NREM sleep

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs of 2-3 Hz rhythmic delta activity lasting 4-10 seconds, occurring every 5-8 minutes during drowsiness
findings.morphology: Monomorphic, semi-rhythmic delta activity with notched morphology

artifacts.impact: Mild, did not significantly obscure epileptiform discharges

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes of hyperventilation performed; mild symmetric increase in background slowing observed. No electrographic seizure activity or activation of epileptiform discharges noted.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal symmetric photic driving response at 8-16 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG due to frequent right anterior temporal spike-and-slow-wave complexes with phase reversal at F8 and intermittent right temporal rhythmic delta activity (TIRDA). The background is otherwise well-organized and symmetric. No electrographic seizures were captured during this 45-minute routine recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute recording. No habitual clinical events were captured. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring is recommended to capture typical events and correlate semiology with electrographic changes.

findings.type: Low-grade neoplasm consistent with dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor (DNET)
findings.location: Right mesial temporal lobe, involving the right amygdala and anterior hippocampus

signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense, well-circumscribed lesion in the right mesial temporal region with a characteristic multicystic or bubbly appearance
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense with a multinodular, bubbly internal architecture. Bright ring sign noted around the periphery of the lesion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense with partial suppression of the cystic components. Surrounding cortex shows subtle increased signal suggesting cortical dysplasia

findings.mass_effect: Minimal. Subtle remodeling of the adjacent temporal horn without obstructive hydrocephalus.
findings.borders: Well-defined, cortically based lesion with sharp margins. No peritumoral edema.

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 3.1 cm3 (lesion partially replaces anterior hippocampal tissue; residual hippocampal volume reduced)
volumetrics.asymmetry_note: Asymmetry due to mass lesion, not atrophic hippocampal sclerosis

additional_observations: No additional parenchymal signal abnormalities in the remainder of the brain
No evidence of hemorrhage or calcification on susceptibility-weighted imaging
Ventricles and sulci otherwise normal in caliber for age
Cerebellar structures and posterior fossa normal
mri.impression: Well-circumscribed, non-enhancing right mesial temporal cortically-based lesion measuring 18 x 14 x 12 mm with bubbly T2-hyperintense morphology involving the right amygdala and anterior hippocampus. No mass effect, no enhancement, no peritumoral edema. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 80 bpm. No arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or ischemic changes identified. No ECG features to suggest a cardiac etiology for the patient's episodic loss of awareness.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: MCV 78.3 fL (L), Prolactin 56.8 ng/mL (H).

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-modulated, reactive
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes. Normal REM sleep recorded.

findings.frequency: Frequent, averaging 15 per hour during wakefulness, increasing to 30 per hour during NREM sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8, maximal negativity at F8/T4, spike duration 30-50 ms with after-going slow wave, field extending to F4
findings.state: All states, maximal during NREM sleep

findings.location: Right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4), evolving to right hemispheric then bilateral
findings.frequency: Three habitual clinical seizures captured over 96 hours
findings.morphology: Seizure onset with right temporal low-amplitude fast activity (beta range) evolving to rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta at F8/T4, then spreading to right hemispheric 3-4 Hz delta over 40 seconds. Third captured seizure secondarily generalized after 65 seconds.
findings.state: Events captured during wakefulness (2 events) and arising from sleep (1 event)

findings.frequency: Intermittent, multiple runs per hour during drowsiness

artifacts.location: Generalized during tonic-clonic phase of third seizure
artifacts.impact: Obscured late ictal EEG during bilateral involvement but onset clearly captured from right temporal region

output.impression: Abnormal 96-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. Three habitual clinical seizures captured, all with clear right anterior temporal electrographic onset (F8/T4). Seizure semiology includes olfactory aura, epigastric rising, behavioral arrest, oral automatisms, and contralateral hand automatisms, concordant with right mesial temporal onset. One event progressed to focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure. Frequent right anterior temporal interictal epileptiform discharges. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Movement artifact during tonic-clonic phase of third seizure limited assessment of late ictal propagation pattern.

findings.type: Cortically-based low-grade neoplasm consistent with DNET
findings.location: Right mesial temporal lobe, centered in the right amygdala with extension into the head of the right hippocampus
findings.size: 19 mm x 15 mm x 13 mm on high-resolution 3T imaging

signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Hypointense, well-circumscribed cortical and subcortical lesion with bubbly, multicystic internal architecture
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Markedly hyperintense with characteristic multinodular appearance on thin coronal cuts (2 mm slices perpendicular to hippocampal long axis). Bright ring sign visible around the cystic components.
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Hyperintense with incomplete suppression of cystic components. Subtle FLAIR signal abnormality in adjacent right temporal neocortex suggesting possible focal cortical dysplasia (type IIIb)
signal_characteristics.SWI: No susceptibility artifact to suggest hemorrhage or calcification
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: No pathological enhancement on post-contrast T1-weighted images

findings.mass_effect: Minimal remodeling of the right temporal horn. No midline shift. No hydrocephalus.
findings.borders: Well-defined, cortically based with sharp margins. No infiltrative pattern.

findings.location: Left hippocampus and remainder of brain parenchyma

signal_characteristics.overall: Normal signal intensity and morphology throughout left hippocampus, bilateral temporal neocortex (excluding right mesial region), frontal lobes, parietal lobes, occipital lobes, basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem, and cerebellum

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 2.9 cm3 (reduced due to partial replacement by lesion)

additional_observations: The adjacent right temporal neocortex shows subtle blurring of the gray-white junction, which may represent associated focal cortical dysplasia (FCD type IIIb as defined by ILAE classification)
No dual pathology in the contralateral hemisphere
Ventricular system otherwise normal in caliber
output.impression: Imaging characteristics are classic for a dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor (DNET). Subtle adjacent cortical dysplasia suggested. Findings concordant with right temporal lobe epilepsy. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.test: Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Long Delay Free Recall)

output.impression: Abnormal values: Full Scale IQ (WAIS-IV) 121 standard score (H), Verbal Comprehension Index 126 standard score (H), Visual Memory (BVMT-R Total Learning) T-score 36 T-score (H), Visual Memory (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 32 T-score (H).

findings.region: Right mesial and lateral temporal lobe
findings.signal: FDG_uptake: Moderately reduced FDG uptake in the right mesial temporal structures corresponding to the known DNET lesion. Mildly reduced uptake extending into the right lateral temporal neocortex, suggesting the epileptogenic zone may extend beyond the lesion margins.; asymmetry_index: Right-to-left temporal lobe asymmetry index 0.81 (normal >0.90), indicating significant right temporal hypometabolism

findings.region: Bilateral frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes; left temporal lobe; basal ganglia; thalami; cerebellum
findings.signal: FDG_uptake: Symmetric and normal FDG uptake throughout the remainder of the brain

output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates right mesial and lateral temporal hypometabolism concordant with the known right mesial temporal DNET and right temporal epileptiform activity on EEG. The hypometabolic zone extends slightly beyond the structural lesion margins into the right lateral temporal neocortex. Multi-modal concordance (MRI, EEG, PET, neuropsychology) strongly supports right temporal lobe epilepsy and favorable surgical candidacy. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.proposed: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily (titrate to 1000-1500 mg twice daily)
warnings: Psychiatric adverse effects (irritability, mood changes, anxiety, depression) reported in approximately 10-15% of patients - counsel patient and monitor
Dose adjustment required in renal impairment (not applicable here, creatinine 0.72 mg/dL)
Monitor for behavioral side effects including irritability and mood changes
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily, titrate slowly over 6-8 weeks to 200 mg daily (slow titration required)
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily, titrate to 150-200 mg twice daily (well tolerated)

output.query: dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor DNET epilepsy surgery outcomes

results.title: Long-term epilepsy surgery outcomes in patients with DNETs
results.summary: Gross total resection of DNETs resulted in Engel class I (seizure-free) outcomes in 85% of patients at long-term follow-up. Incomplete resection was the main predictor of seizure recurrence.

results.title: Surgical treatment of epilepsy associated with dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumors
results.source: Bilginer et al., Childs Nerv Syst 2009;25:1231-1237
results.summary: Complete lesionectomy with resection of surrounding epileptogenic cortex yielded seizure freedom in 80-90% of cases. Histopathological confirmation is essential for prognosis.

results.title: DNETs in adults: surgical outcomes and histopathological features
results.source: Lee et al., J Clin Neurosci 2018;56:74-79
results.summary: Adult patients with DNETs have excellent surgical outcomes comparable to pediatric cohorts. Duration of epilepsy prior to surgery did not significantly affect seizure-free rates, though earlier intervention is generally recommended.

output.summary: DNETs are WHO grade I tumors with an excellent prognosis following complete surgical resection. Seizure freedom rates of 80-90% are consistently reported with gross total resection. The key surgical consideration is complete removal of both the lesion and any associated focal cortical dysplasia. Lesion-specific surgery (lesionectomy with corticectomy) and standard anterior temporal lobectomy both produce favorable outcomes in temporal lobe DNETs. These tumors do not undergo malignant transformation and do not require adjuvant chemoradiation.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (right mesial temporal lobe epilepsy secondary to dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor)

differential.key_features: Semiology is classic for temporal lobe seizures with stereotyped olfactory aura, epigastric rising, behavioral arrest, oral automatisms, and secondary generalization. Elevated post-ictal prolactin and clear EEG epileptiform abnormalities argue strongly against PNES.

No prodrome of lightheadedness, pallor, or diaphoresis. Prolonged post-ictal confusion is inconsistent with syncope. Normal ECG. The duration of tonic-clonic activity (2 minutes) far exceeds the brief myoclonic jerks (5-10 seconds) that may accompany syncope.

Migraine aura spreads gradually over 5-60 minutes and is typically visual or somatosensory. The patient's episodes are brief (seconds), involve olfactory hallucination and epigastric sensation, and progress to impaired awareness - a temporal lobe seizure pattern, not migraine.

While the patient initially attributed events to panic, classic panic attacks do not feature stereotyped olfactory hallucinations, behavioral arrest with oral automatisms, or post-ictal confusion. The brief, stereotyped, involuntary nature of the episodes with clear EEG correlate establishes an epileptic etiology.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including seizure semiology from both the patient and the witness, with attention to aura features, automatisms, duration, and post-ictal state
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Olfactory aura with epigastric rising followed by behavioral arrest, oral automatisms, left hand automatisms, and focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure with prolonged post-ictal confusion
Normal interictal neurological exam with evidence of lateral tongue bite from the witnessed seizure
Right anterior temporal spike-and-slow-wave complexes and TIRDA, consistent with right temporal epileptogenic focus

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain with epilepsy protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right mesial temporal cortically-based lesion consistent with DNET involving amygdala and anterior hippocampus

optimal_actions.action: Obtain basic laboratories including BMP, CBC, prolactin, TSH, and urine drug screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic panel, elevated post-ictal prolactin, negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to rule out cardiac etiology
Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy appropriate for focal epilepsy with structural lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Levetiracetam or lamotrigine selected with no significant drug interactions with current medications

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient on driving restrictions and seizure safety precautions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient informed of state-specific driving laws, need to avoid heights and swimming alone

optimal_actions.action: Refer to comprehensive epilepsy center for surgical evaluation given structural lesion (DNET) associated with medically intractable potential
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Early surgical referral placed, as DNETs are highly amenable to curative resection with 80-90% seizure-free outcomes

critical_actions: Obtain EEG to characterize epileptiform activity and lateralize the epileptogenic focus
Obtain brain MRI with epilepsy protocol to identify the structural lesion (DNET)
Obtain basic metabolic panel and CBC to exclude metabolic causes of seizure
Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy appropriate for focal epilepsy with structural lesion
Counsel patient on driving restrictions and seizure safety precautions
Refer to epilepsy surgery center given structural lesion with high likelihood of surgical cure
Document seizure semiology including aura to support localization
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing enzyme-inducing AEDs (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital) as first-line without considering side effect profiles and drug interactions
Performing lumbar puncture in this clinical scenario (no indication for CSF analysis; no signs of infection, inflammation, or elevated intracranial pressure)
Discharging patient without initiating antiepileptic therapy after a focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure with a structural brain lesion
Prescribing bupropion, which lowers seizure threshold
Advising that the patient can continue driving immediately after a seizure
Ordering brain biopsy as a first step - the MRI characteristics of DNET are sufficiently characteristic, and surgical resection (not biopsy) is the appropriate intervention if surgery is pursued
key_reasoning_points: Olfactory hallucinations (unpleasant burning smell) are a localizing aura pointing to the mesial temporal lobe, particularly the amygdala and uncus, which is concordant with the lesion location

The prior brief stereotyped episodes of olfactory hallucination and epigastric rising represent focal aware seizures that were unrecognized by the patient, indicating chronic epilepsy rather than a first-ever seizure
The bubbly, non-enhancing, cortically-based MRI appearance in a young patient with chronic seizures is classic for DNET, a WHO grade I tumor with an excellent prognosis after complete resection
Cross-modal concordance is present: right temporal EEG focus, right mesial temporal MRI lesion, right temporal PET hypometabolism, and visual memory deficit lateralizing to the right temporal lobe
The choice of AED should consider the patient's comorbidities and potential drug interactions
DNETs are associated with focal cortical dysplasia (FCD type IIIb by ILAE classification), and resection of both the lesion and surrounding dysplastic cortex improves seizure-free surgical outcomes
Left-handedness raises the possibility of atypical (right hemisphere or bilateral) language dominance, which should be evaluated with fMRI or Wada testing before right temporal surgery

metadata.estimated_complexity: Straightforward - classic presentation with concordant structural lesion and EEG findings
